title: InkDrop
description: InkDrop started as a simple library idea and became a full request-to-fulfillment system with moderation and role-aware workflows.

content.text: InkDrop began with a very real pain: students paying too much for books they needed only briefly.
My first version was basically upload and download. It looked fine until moderation, access control, and request ownership started colliding in real usage.
I redesigned it around two loops: user discovery and requests, plus admin review and fulfillment. That flow made the product understandable and maintainable.
Visibility of request status changed user trust more than any visual enhancement.
I moved file handling to object storage boundaries and kept metadata in MongoDB. I also added debounced search to stop backend spam from half-typed queries.
The hardest engineering problem was not uploads. It was safe workflow transitions under human behavior.
Admin collision bugs pushed me to enforce strict status transitions and idempotent processing so stale actions could not re-run the same request path.
InkDrop changed my product mindset from feature-first to flow-first. Discover, request, approve, fulfill, and trust must work as one chain.

content.suggestion: Request timeline from user submission through admin approval to fulfilled status
